I had come to this seminar. I was so excited that I got to just relax, be like everybody else. I had no responsibility. I didn't have to do anything until Dan saw me this morning. And he said, I have something for you to do. But he did not put it here. So what we're going to do is we're going to get ready and be entertained by five videos. So in your workbook, if you would go to six videos, thank you very much. I know this is five here. Great. Go to page 58. Okay. On 58, you can start to take some notes. So the first video is going to be two newsreels of Houdini doing his famous straight jacket escape. The second video is going to be how to demonstrate any intangible, and this is Dan's demo from the Daily Cash in Your Mailbox infomercial. The third and fourth video are going to be from Goal by the Inch infomercial, which holds the record for the longest consecutive airing of pure lead generation show. Every day, somewhere in the United States for eight solid years. And both of these are going to be tracking the challenges of demonstrating an intangible. Now I am not seeing this, but I will, I might be embarrassed here because I used to do some of those infomercials. And just so you know, if I did them, I was like 18 or so. So give me a little break. If you believe that, I have some really good land for you. The next video is going to be from the reality show Pitchman, which is a very dramatic demonstration. So we are going to enjoy ourselves with videos for a little while. So roll it. Would you please bring in the mail? Set it right down here. Want to bring the table over? Now folks, what I have done is for the last couple of days, just save the mail from my office and had it shipped here so that I could have it opened up right before you to see what it is like to receive daily cash in the mail. And look at this. This is mail from all over the country. We are not going to open it all up because it will be all over everywhere. Now listen, I need some volunteers. First of all, is there anyone that knows how to operate a calculator very quickly? Okay sir, would you please come down? And then I need, thank you. And I need some other people that will come and help me feel this excitement of opening daily cash. You, you, okay and you sir? Come on down. Come on down here. Okay, now what I want you to do is as quickly as you can is open up the envelope and just reach in and take the checkout, keep it with the envelope and then what's your name sir? Andre. Pass it over to Andre so that he can start adding it up, okay? Now listen to this folks. Okay here's a check for $39.95. How much is your check for? $39.95. Okay we got $39.95. $39.95. Okay, another one for $39. $40. $40. $39.95. Okay another one for $39. Okay listen to this folks. $39.95. $10.00. $30.30. $40. $50. $10. $4. $2. $10. $10. $12. $13. $14. $13. $13. $14. $24. $14. $14. $14. $15. $10. $1. $24. $15. And once you are done, you can now go to the whistling table at your nearest address. mail box. How much? $89.25. Andre, how much money do we have? So far we've got $600. $600. And just a few minutes and open daily cash. This is a demonstration of how to sell your jewelry. It is based on the most common questions you will be asked and intended to be only a basic sales presentation. Can you tell me what this is? It's 14 karat gold layer. What does that mean? It means that 14 karat gold has been layered over a semi-precious jeweler's metal. Will it wear off? No, most people wear it for years without any visible change. However, it does come with a manufacturer's lifetime guarantee. If at any time you have any problems with a chain, including breaking a clasp, just send it back directly to US Gold Chain and they will replace it at no charge except for postage and handling. Can you make a necklace at any length I want? I can make one at the exact length while you wait. I can do the same for bracelets and anklets. When you buy a chain by the inch, you will always have a custom fit. How much is it? It's sold by the inch, so you only pay for what you need. I'll measure the chain and tell you how much it costs. This measure is 23 inches. This chain sells for $2 per inch. Your cost is $46. Let me give you an idea of how much a matching bracelet would cost. Your wrist measures only 6 inches. At $2 per inch, your cost is only $12. You'd never be able to buy a ready-made bracelet that is only 6 inches. I can also put a charm on the necklace for you. Why don't you browse through the selection of 200 charms? If you find one you like, I'll put it on the necklace while you're waiting. Oh, okay. I'll take the bracelet and the necklace and pick out a charm for the necklace while you're making up the chain. What do you think about that chain? Oh, it's super. You know that they can make this up exactly to the length I need for my new dress. Is that right? Yeah. And tell me, sir, what do you think about this chain? Well, I'm just amazed at the price. I think that it's reasonable that you should buy two. You should buy two of them. Isn't that nice? Wonderful. So, I'm leaping to page 58. And we're going to talk about demonstration. The Sam Pitts show, what we originally wanted to do, by the way, was have a real US Postal Service truck drive on and postal guys take the mail out and the Postal Service wouldn't do it. So, God forbid they should have free advertising. But I still think that was a very cool demonstration for a very hard thing to demonstrate. So, your page 58, which I have lost the white version of it, but you don't really need it. There are basically five kinds of demonstration that we can do. We can do the demonstration of profound importance. Did you show Houdini? Okay. Okay. So, the thing about that Houdini demo you know, it's the straight jacket escape, which is a straight jacket escape is a straight jacket escape is a straight jacket escape. It's actually easier to do it hanging from a crane from the distance where nobody can get a good look at you than it is to do it right here on stage standing in front of you. But think how much cooler it is to see it while you are hanging from a crane. So, there's a way to do demonstrations that make them much more important that do not really affect the nature of the demo. If you're going to show a video clip of you getting off of an airplane, I didn't dredge this out to show it from the old Glenn Turner film, but so you're going to show yourself getting off of your plane. Here's how to make that much more important. There's a crowd of 100 people there waiting for you to get off the plane. That's what they do for politicians. Politicians flying into a city in order to go raise some money and they round up 100 people and take them out so the news cameras see a big crowd waiting for the person to get off the plane. You might have a crowd you can pull together. You can always rent a crowd, but if you're going to show that anyway, you might as well show it in a way that's important. Product performance. So, the Sam Pitts example is a way to do product performance even when we don't have a product like that that actually performs. Personal wizardry. So, in our world, that's often done with hot seats. So, Jay Abraham, Gary Halver, me, we will demonstrate the wizardry by doing the hot seat and do four or five people and do their businesses. It's important to pick them. You do not just let anybody stand up and come up and do the hot seat. If you're doing it for this purpose, it's important to screen them and pick them in advance. When the faith healer smacks them on the head and they can walk and dance again, they were pre-chosen. It's not like anybody who wants to. They screen them back there and they let them come out because they have to be susceptible. They have to be, and in our case, if we're going to do a hot seat, they have to be like sane and rational and have something that actually can be helped. You know, you don't want, when you have no control over it, then it backfires. I had, I did it at a, I mentioned it yesterday, I did it at a Dottie Walder seminar for speakers and authors. And so she wanted me to do hot seats and I didn't get a chance to screen them. So, the first one is a very nice lady who has written six books of poetry that are now stacked up in her garage. And she wants to be the next chicken soup for the soul with her books of poetry that are already stacked up in her garage and think associations are going to sponsor her and hire her. And I was much younger. I wasn't as nice as I am now. But so I did a legitimate hot seat, you know, and she ran from the room weeping and was curled up outside with people consoling her and it cast a pall over the rest of the, you know, my version of it in the Peter Lo speech was of course, the Giorgio letters demonstration. So we read parts of them, we showed you how the three step letter sequence works. A lot of people haven't seen it because I only get it when I did three hour long magnetic marketing presentations, which were mostly association speaking gigs. But I would have on stage, I would have a table with a couple of chairs. And I would fairly early bring a couple people up out of the audience. And I would have a fill in the blank template right out of the magnetic marketing system of the customer reactivation letter. And I would sit them down at the table and have them fill in the blanks about their business right in front of everybody with no preparation. And then I would show their letters, thereby demonstrating that you can take this home. And you don't have to know anything. All you have to do is understand your business and you can fill in these blanks just like mad libs and bingo, you have a letter ready to go. And I couldn't do it in the timeframe I had at Peter's, but I could do it. If I had 300 insurance agents that are Florida State Association insurance meeting. There's demonstration, obviously of safety in numbers, pictures, video, huge crowds. There is a belief for presenters, speakers, that if they speak to big audiences, they are more important, smarter and more valuable than if they speak to small audiences. Obviously, stupid. But still, people see video or photo of you. So you'll notice, in all of our GKIC stuff, we have this one wide angle shot that of the audience from the back. And it's shot like in an arc, like the new curved TVs. So it makes it look even bigger than it really is. Not any smarter because I'm standing in front of that, that if I was standing in front of 12 people, but people think there are. And demonstration of happy outcomes, which mostly has to be done through social proof and lifestyle visuals and so forth. But those are the five kinds of demonstration. The more of it you do, if you are doing one to many presentations that you are delivering live or by video, the more demonstration you do, the better that you will do. So page, where are we? 77. Structure of promises. I'm still in the middle of the presentation, by the way, just in case anybody got a little lost. And so, how do we structure promises? So the first issue is how big a promise are you going to make? I mentioned yesterday, I have two corporate clients, they both had this conversation very recently with me of, gee, can't we scale down the promises? Can't we back off the promises? Yeah, as long as you're willing to sell a lot less, you can. But otherwise, people don't just want to lose weight, they want to lose weight while they sleep after having eaten everything they ever wanted to eat. That's how people want to lose weight. So the bigger the promise, the bigger the result. And so there's a huge difference. I have examples for you here. These are all actually real examples. So these were all split tested. So there's an enormous difference between, and it should be obvious, learn the secrets of people who never get sick versus learn how to boost your immune system. Now, if you let the academics write it, or you let the lawyers write it, they will write the second one. If you have any doubt in a split test, it's the first one that wins. Grow twice the garden for half the cost and make your neighbors green with envy. 50 tips for a more bountiful garden. Obviously, we know which one wins. The first one even has the prime benefit in it, which is sticking it up the butt of the neighbor. So big promise versus small promise, you want to structure the biggest promise that you feel you can fulfill and won't put you in jail. The bigger the bolder the better direct promises versus implied promises. So there's a way to do promises that are direct. If you are willing to do them, make $4,000 a day sitting at your kitchen table and going to work. That's a direct promise. And it has issues. The implied promise version of it is there are people making amazing incomes with a new kitchen table business. Now, it's not as strong, but it does not say you will, or you can, or even you can potentially. It just states a fact and requires you to translate in your head, gee, if there are people, then I could. You have to decide whether you want to make a direct promise or whether you feel you need to make an implied promise. Another way to do implied promises that you will see done a lot is implied promises framed as questions. So in 1977, being a millionaire in America was still a big deal. And so this was a headline in some copy from an ad I wrote that was extremely effective. And the question, the headline is you, a millionaire. And there's statements of fact. And then there's questions. Nowhere did I make a direct promise. I didn't say you could be a millionaire, would be a millionaire, might be a millionaire, potentially could be a millionaire. I raised questions and made factual statements. Is it as strong as no, but can't so this is this is one of a WAIs. Can you write a simple sales letter? Answer yes, and you could be in demand earning great money writing just so that's across. Have you ever noticed there seems to be some people who can eat whatever they want as much as they want and never gain weight. It's an implied promise. But we didn't say you could. We just said, Have you ever noticed? There are primary promises and secondary promises. So the primary promise is sales professionals, you can put an end to cold prospecting once and for all. Now the secondary promises tend to get at what does that mean to different people who may be paying attention to the primary promise. So if we were back in the open, the only thing you would have time to do is the primary promise. In the middle, if we properly bought time for ourselves, we have time to develop the idea. And so we can deliver the secondary promises, one of which has to do with time, one of which has to do with money, one of which has to do with envy, one of which has to do with state of mind, and one of which has to do with family. So there's there's one, two, three, four, five. Yeah, there's five secondary promises for the primary promise. I will tend usually to do three to five. So here's a couple of ways to think this through as you go to structure it. We spare no expense. So one is primary promise, actual secondary benefits, how I feel because of it, secondary benefits, and how others feel about me because of it, secondary benefits. So this is a how others feel about me secondary benefit. Your spouse, kids and friends will even notice the difference in you. The people that sell you can be home for dinner kind of thing. Ben does it, Craig does it, you know, it's your dog will actually recognize you and not bite you. See, that's a that's in the third column here. That's how others feel about me because I have bought this on this do this. So there's three kinds of secondary benefits that you can build actual ones, how the buyer feels and how others feel about the buyer. And this is not your book. Another way to think about and think it through is the old wheel. You know, this is like used to sell coaching. So there's primary promise. And then here's the different ways you can do secondary pen promises. There could be a money one. There could be a love sex one. There could be a family one. There could be a health one. There could be a security one. There could be a status one and there can be a lifestyle one. You only need one of each and you're over five. So primary, secondary. Another promise structure is what's called stacking promises. And this is important when you're time tight. So if you're time tight in a presentation, like an infomercial, or you really, for whatever reason, can only do a seven and a half minute video, and that's what you could bitch yourself you can do. Then trying to stack two or three promises into one concise statement is what you want to do. Book titles often have to do this. Hence, Napoleon Hill's book title grow rich with peace of mind. Stack title, right? Win friends and influence people. Stacked title. Infusions conquer the chaos how to one grow a successful small business to without going crazy. Stacked title. The same thing when you write headlines when you write promise statements, when you write claims, you can concisely stack one, two or three all within one sentence. When you are time tight, the converse truth of this is if you waste a sentence delivering only one promise, you probably could have delivered two promises in the same sentence. If you sat and thought about it and worked at it. Also remember that what is it what something isn't constitutes promise is a structure of promise. And is often as powerful or influential as saying what something is. You can't resource for you, by the way. Another one of Masterson's books. If you don't own the architecture of persuasion, cheap book, you can get it at Amazon. It will help you with promise structure. It's architecture of persuasion, excuse me, by Michael Masterson. So now let's talk about evergreen pitches. So yesterday we talked a little bit about evergreen assets, by the way. So if you're going to go to the trouble to build a pitch and you're going to go to the trouble to build a group sales presentation that you are going to deliver however it is, you're going to deliver it. You might as well base it on some of the appeals that never lose their punch that are literally evergreen. They work at the turn of the century. They'll work at the turn of the next century. They'll be working long after we're gone. They fall into a fairly limited number of categories. One is money, financial opportunity. And the deliverable maybe changes. So you guys probably remember the little screaming guy in the infomercial with you can make money with little tiny classified ads. See that same pitch today is for you can make money with Craigslist. There was a decade of very popular how to make money taking stuff to swap meets and selling it. That now is how to make money on eBay. It's the same pitch, though. And so it's important also when you build your pitch today, not just to narrow your range of knowledge to what's happened in the last five minutes. Because if you will dredge historically in the category you're in, you will find the evergreen threads that have been in these pitches again and again and again and again. So as classified ads have gone away, Craigslist is here. Same pitch when Craigslist goes away, whatever the hell replaces it, it'll be the same pitch. So financial is pretty much evergreen. Never goes away. People always want more money. They are not necessarily willing to work for more money, but they always want more money. You will hardly ever get a hand raised if you take a survey or a poll. Do you have all the money you want and you don't want anymore? You may notice most of the billionaires in the country are 70 and 80 years old and they're working. They don't need money, but they're still keeping score by money. And if you put them all in a room and said, you need more money, most of them would say yes. And the ones that didn't say yes to need, if you said you want more money, they would say we want more money. Weight loss. Never goes away. People were fat, are fat, will be fat forever more. And now it's global because we're exporting our food. So, well, I'm serious. A decade ago, try and find me a fat Chinese person in China. Now the place is full of them. They're eating KFC, they're eating Taco Bell, they're eating Pizza Hut. They're fat. You can see them because they all come to Disney. So, I'm serious. I'm serious. So it used to be you went to Disney and everybody from a foreign country was skinny and everybody from our country was the wide load people. Now everybody's a wide load person. You see them from the back and you think, well, there's an American. And you like finally get past them and you turn around and you look at son of a gun. They look like they're from India. How can they be fat? Well, because we're exporting all our shit. That's why. So weight loss now used to be really big here and not very big anywhere else. That was big everywhere. And I do mean big everywhere. So weight loss, right? Pitches haven't changed, by the way, the deliverable changes. Embarrassing and adequacy solved. Evergreen pitch. Everybody's very insecure. They're easily agitated to be more insecure about almost anything and everything. So embarrassing and adequacy solved has been around since the beginning of time and it will continue to be around. How many have read the the great biography that came out late last year on Dale Carnegie on the whole Dale Carnegie business? Do you remember the title? Because I'm American. Thank you, self help Messiah. Okay, so a bunch of hands didn't go up. It's worth reading no matter what business you're in, by the way, but if you happen to be in the information business, it's like mandatory reading. So it's a very it's academic. It's not exactly the most exciting thing you're ever going to read. But it's a very well researched, very good book on what is the longest enduring most successful self help thing in America, Dale Carnegie. And the whole empire was all about shyness and timidity and a sense of social inadequacy and in business communication inadequacy. So it's an inadequacy play proactive. My client's $800 million a year business is an inadequacy play. It's not as much about what the kid sees or the adult sufferer sees. You look in the mirror for their own purposes. It's about feeling horrible about having acne because of what other people think. Listerine's ad copywriters in 1922 invented bad breath. There was no such thing. It is not a medical condition. It was created by an advertising copywriter trying to sell a liquid that tastes like shit. So they invented bad breath. If you by the way, there's a ton of those historical examples. Body odor was invented by an advertising copywriter. I don't remember them all and I didn't go research them all. But pretty much if you name any embarrassing condition, it was invented by the advertising copywriter charged with selling the solution. It had not been previously even thought about body odor up until 1948 in this country, the average person took a shower or bath once a week. That that was our social norm. Friday night baths that was the social norm in America started to change right around 1950. So people really weren't concerned about body odor. Until an advertising guy made them concerned about body odor. You've all read have you all read the making them believe book at least if you haven't got the course. Yes, yes, yes, yes, yes. Anybody not should if you have to be in health care, you definitely should. So I mean, Brinkley really was selling the first cure for erectile dysfunction. And that is all about sense of inadequacy. So this is this is an evergreen one. So if you think about Brinkley, so that was 19. What 1906 1910 somewhere in that neighborhood, right? So that business opportunity hasn't gone anywhere. Right. It is bigger than ever. Oh, come on. That that see that kind of thing don't happen by accident that I have to think about that. Okay, so here this is a current magazine ad. It's had at least a three year run without a change is a very successful product. This is a huge industry. And it's never going to thank you. There you say if I keep beating the horse, you'll eventually, you know, you'll get it. Superiority evergreen pitch. Okay. Martha Stewart exists because of the superiority appeal. I can be superior to my other women friends. If I have a Martha Stewart luncheon, and I cut on the trees and I make the napkins. And they're in shapes of the lulls and etc. etc. That's what that is. Okay. Cosmopolitan magazine. I don't think you have these. I don't believe there's no page number on it. So I don't believe you have it. So Cosmopolitan magazine created its cover and headline format. Right. In 1962. In 2014, the headline, the cover, the one I just so glommed in Barnes and Noble took a look at one of the headlines is you'll how to give him the best sex he's ever had. Men have finally bit. So Maxim mind blowing orgasm. She'll never forget. And no man will ever top. You can go back to 1962 find the same stuff on the cover. Cosmopolitan hasn't changed. For men, instructions real for women. Pretty much all you got to do show up naked. That's it. This is not rocket science in order to Starbucks is actually a superiority play. People who are regular Starbucks customers feel superior to schmucks who buy their coffee at Dunkin Donuts and at the convenience store. It's a superiority play. It's because see this is about because I buy this I am this. That's what this is about. It's status by purchase. Right. We'll never go away. Disaster prep. I just had lunch with Harry Denton. Because Harry said if you want anybody to come scare them, I'm your guy. I can scare the shit out. So disaster prep has never gone away. Little known little known little known amway fact. In 1959 and 1960 and we had three products. They had a liquid cleaner. They had laundry soap and they had backyard bomb shelters. Those are the three things they were selling. And they actually sold a fairly good number of them. And you can buy backyard bomb shelters today. They're much more expensive and they come and install them and they stock them with food and air and light and water and I'm not I've never understood the concept. Because when you finally emerge. Now what? It's all gone. You have now consumed all the water all the food and you now emerge to a crater. The only other people are left to people who had bomb shelters. They too have used everything up. So it seems to me the biggest guy is going to eat the smallest weakest guy that's sort of the plan. But gold. So gold kind of goes away a little bit in cycles. But it always comes out every time there's a bad economy. Why so you know guns. So I always say you know if you if you visit somebody you the way to tell if they're a Glenn Becklister is when they raise their garage door. Half of the garage is full of gold and half of the garage is full of guns. And there's a guy sitting in a lawn chair with a gun in his lap. Garden and if they're not full of guns it's full of survival food. So now you got to Glenn Becklister. OK. It's a huge market hasn't changed won't go away. It ebbs and flows a little bit with conditions. But it never really goes away. An end of America huge hit against an enemy evergreen category. As long as somebody has an enemy. There will be stuff they buy because of that enemy. So if you go back and look at the four thousand dollars a day in your underwear ad in the archives collection you'll find we got a big bump as soon as we put a paragraph at the bottom of it about Hillary coming to get your money. Now it would be about Obama coming to get your money. And in however many years it is. I have a countdown thing in my office but I don't have it in my head. When whoever it is it takes a place it will be about that person coming to get your money and it will still get the bump. So enemy and then clever combinations thereof. This by the way is the current end of America piece. And so this is the same pitch. I just want to show you how little it takes to update anything. So this is the same end of America pitch that a Gorgia that Stansbury is now used for three years. The direct mail piece. I want to show you the current changes. The make it current one. He's picked a new date for the end of America. Okay. Because lo and behold it didn't end on the last three dates. So now it's July 1st 2014 which by the way you make a point. Laugh really enjoy this seminar because July 1st baby it's all over right. And there's a new sticker on it which really isn't a sticker. It's just printed to look like a sticker. It's got a picture of Obama our enemy. And it says new Obama law first look new currency law goes into effect July 1st. Right. And it appears in two places. The rest of this is unchanged. Same pitch. Unchanged since the beginning. And they're still mailing a crap out of it and it's still working. So those are your categories of evergreen appeals. And you ought to consider them when you go build any presentation. You might as well hook it on. Build it on a foundation on one of the appeals that never dies. That is reliable when you structure a presentation. Now I'm back to your manual on page 81. Those of you that were grumpy about it. So I call this the lost secret of seven to 21 times. So we used to get this. Everybody in my business understood it about the training side of our business. So we always sold with it to the seven to 21 times repetition. So if you move into a new house like an idiot. So this is part of the pitch to self cassette programs. Because you are going to go listen to them seven to 21 times. Which then you did a value proposition by the way. So there's six cassettes in an album but you have to listen to each one seven to 21 times. So even if you only do seven that's 42. So if you divide $60 by 42 it's a lot less than it is if you divide $60 by six. So it's part of the value proposition. And the reason that it's important is because if you move into a new house then depending upon how quick a study you are. But at least for seven nights like an idiot. You will be in the dark reaching for the light switches where they were in the old house. Most people will be reaching for the light switches where they were in the old house for 21 nights in a row before their hand and mind readjusts and automatically goes to the light switch where it is in a new house. Therefore you need seven to 21. So you need to have a good understanding of the repetitions of any input that you want to actually stick and be held and be able to be used. Okay so that was the pitch. So we all understood it. Well most people don't know is that it was a good pitch. It's legitimate. It's actually true. So well it's not necessary from the standpoint of a pitch. But it is actually true. It is how we get things. We get them through repetition. We get them even when we don't try. So everybody in the room knows lyrics to some song. You made no attempt whatsoever to memorize. It's not like you sat there and tried to memorize it. But you know them. And at least with a prompt you can probably do them. It's why it's why name that tune works. You can hear the first few bars of a song and you know what the song is. You didn't try and do that. It happened to you automatically through repetition. So what happens automatically by accident without you trying to do it. If you just apply it to what you're trying to do. This is how I got through Latin in high school. Which by the way so the first dumb mistake is taking Latin. Some vague thought that I wanted to be a veterinarian and at the time you know you still needed Latin to get through vet school or medical school. It was not that I was planning on being a priest. I was planning on being a veterinarian. And we had Spanish, French and Latin and the hottest teacher was Latin. So there was two there was two means of making that decision. It turns out to be completely useless. Every once in a while I can get covatus into a conversation. But but so here's how I get through Latin. So Latin our system anyway you had a weekly quiz and you had a monthly test. The weekly quiz was vocabulary. English word Latin word Latin word English word can be memorized. The monthly test you had to use the words in present tense past tense future tense in sentences. The grade quizzes counted for half the grade test counted for half the grade. That means if you ace the quizzes, you could flunk the test and get a C. That's me. The memorization was recording the vocabulary and then playing it on an audio cassette over and over and over again until the quiz, including while I slept with a little pillow speaker. Ace every single quiz. Flunked every single test. Just sat there because I had an idea. Three or four times accused of cheating on the quizzes because how could you ace the quiz and flunk the test. I kept explaining to them because to do the test you have to be able to use this crap. To ace the quiz you only have to know it. I know it. I memorized it. I just can't use it. So our minds really do work this way. Stuff sticks through repetition. So when you build a presentation, you want seven to 21 repetitions of the most important things in them internally during the course of the presentation. So if you go back through the script samples I've given you and you hit upon a big idea, an important point made early and now you stick count how many times it's restated almost identically during the course of that presentation, you're going to hit seven to 21. I don't miss because I make sure I don't miss. So how do you say it seven to 21 times in a presentation without being horribly clumsy about it. Although better to be horribly clumsy about it than not do it. One, you build it into your very open. You build it into your open tees, your open promise, your open assertions as early in the presentation as you possibly can. You put the most important thing you want to drive home that has to be accepted in order for them to buy. You can say it by direct assertion. You can bury it inside a demonstration and have an opportunity to say it again, show it, prove it. You can bury it inside testimonials. So the testimonials either say it or they get close to it or they reinforce it. So you can deliver it inside testimonials. You can put it inside a story. You can put it inside the headline of a story. So the way you announce a story or start a story allows you to say it again. So far, big idea is big money and big chunks. We can put it every place I've named so far. We can put it in an opening tees. We can say it as a direct assertion. With this system, you'll get big money and big chunks. We can put it in some kind of demonstration. We could have made all those checks he took out of the envelopes, $5,000 checks instead of $19 checks. We have demonstrated big money and big chunks. We can have testimonials say I never thought only rich people and doctors and lawyers got big money and big chunks. But now, so we have testimonials say the headline of the story. Here's the story of a desperate Iowa housewife who discovered how to get big money and big chunks. And then we tell her story. As the punch line of the story, at the end, as you can see, Judy discovered how to get big money and big chunks. We can tie it to a proffer of facts. So if this is, if our big idea is get your teeth three shades wider and just three days, we can tie it to the fact. So the Harvard study showed that 9 out of 10 dentists recommend X, which is exactly why you can rely on X, which is, they didn't say you could, which is exactly why. So if we say it's exactly why, or if I say, okay, that's exactly why you can rely on X to get your teeth three shades wider in just three days. We can obviously put it inside the guarantee. If you don't get big money and big chunks within 89 days, you can return everything. So we can put it in the guarantee and we can stick it in a trial clause. So there's 10 ways we can try and get our repetition within the presentation. And they, it's not the same as just repeating it over and over and over again. Now, one to many at a distance, and even in a room, is handicapped by the fact that often you are speed selling, and they do not have a firm grip on what it is they are buying. There is an impulse to the purchase. Right, a guy, he bought over the years at the success events, he bought magnetic marketing nine times. He didn't realize it until, like, the 10th year. And he's telling me, I bought it in 1996, I bought it in 1998, I bought it from, he was moving. And finally, it all came together, all still shrink wrapped of course. But he just got caught up in the pitch and ran to the back of the room and bought it, never once thinking, I was here three years ago, same guy, same suit, same slides, I wonder if it's the same product, right? So, so they were, were speed selling, they're buying on impulse, they often don't know exactly what they're buying. That's somewhat of a handicap. So we got to implant the big idea that drives the purchase through repetition. There's also a thing about it being a closed loop. So, so how many have read the book, sham? Oh, not near enough. Okay, I forget the author, doesn't make any difference. And I will preface it by saying it's only about 70% right. And in places it is profoundly unfair. But it is still, really everybody ought to read it, just kind of substitute what goes on in your industry to this. If you are in any way, shape or form, my industry, that's what it's written about. So it is a attack on, it is an attack on the self help industry. That's what it is. So it's a little dated now. Some, but Tony's in it and Barbara d'Angeles is in it and John Gray, you know, the Venus Mars guy. So there's a lot of people you know, who are, who are critically featured in the book. And so sham stands for self help, something movement is what it's an abbreviation for. So the reason to read it beyond fun, if you have sort of a perverse idea of fun, is that he does accurately reveal a lot of the structural stuff about how self help motivation and success is sold to people. And his background is he was at Rodale for many years as a copywriter. So he was at Rodale, it'd be like being at boardroom, you know, whatever. So you get the book, it's in its name is Steve Salerno, S-A-L-E-R-N-O. You can get the book, it's paperbacked and get it at Amazon. So one of the things that he reveals is there is a circular closed loop to this structure. So I'll just show you. So this is his criticism. This is not to say it's false, there are self help precepts that can't be disputed because they are circular. The guru is saying the same thing in different ways at the start and end of the sentence. The conclusion restates the promise. So if you start to get analytical about this, you will see it done again and again and again. The conclusion proves the promise and the conclusion is only a restatement of the promise. So here's a key words for example are that being true. So here's a sham example. Incredibly most sales professionals choose to be miserable while a few choose to prosper and enjoy selling. Now jump to the end. I won't read the middle first. That fact guarantees that you can choose freedom from prospecting or drudgery while others choose misery. It's a big circle. We're just going around in a circle. So now read the whole thing. Incredibly most sales professionals choose to be miserable while a few choose to prosper and enjoy selling. There's nothing more discouraging to sales professionals than endless difficult cold prospecting. Most simply resign themselves to it but those who discover magnetic marketing break free of it never waste a minute. Be an annoying pester set of a welcome guest. Again that's how you two can transform your experience in selling. That fact, what fact? Be analytical for a second. What fact? That fact guarantees that you could choose freedom from prospecting drudgery while others choose misery which is a restatement of the first sense of the paragraph. It's a, oh yeah it's beautiful. Thank you very much. It's a self proving circular piece of language that didn't go anywhere and didn't really establish anything. So when you build your 7 to 21 repetitions you can build them as closed loops which actually gets you another repetition. Because you are proving the promise with a restatement of the promise and you are calling the middle that said nothing the fact that proves the promise. When you go to repeat and reinforce you can do it verbally right. So somehow it's done verbally. You can do it visually, live or video right. And you can do it in print if you have print. By the way I showed you in a split test yesterday an online order form where the only change they made was adding two little tiny little testimonials to a little blank space left at the bottom of the order form. Again I have gotten a little lazy about it over the years but it's amazing how many order forms don't have any testimonials on them. Particularly speakers who use them live. My Peter Loe form had testimonials on the back. If they are going to stand in line waiting and they are thinking maybe they are going to walk away. They might as well read a testimonial and the testimonials reinforce the key promises. So there are a few rules sort of. You do have this. I am all the way up to 84. One is you want to bookend one way or another. Because the law primacy of recites he says most people if you bring them up on stage and you give them a list of six random numbers. One, forty-eight, twelve, nine. Most people can remember the first couple and the last couple. They can't get the ones in the middle. And if they get the ones in the middle they get them in the wrong order. It's called primacy and recency. So in the longer the presentation the more problematic this is. Because they can only remember what you started with and what you are ending with and they can't remember anything in the middle. But it's even true of a short paragraph. So you want to bookend and bookend and bookend and bookend when you do your repetition. Sometimes you are going to have to stop and summarize that points within a long presentation. You have to bring people. So if you made five promises, so you will see in my, I will get to it, you will see in the magnetic marketing speech. So I had three acts. I had three things that we talked about. Message, market, media. There is a summary statement. Okay, now we have got message taken care of. Now we have message and market. Now we will go to beat. So you have to stop and put what you have done together for them. So okay, now I got all four of those things. It has a primacy. Now he is going to talk again and he is going to stop with a point. And so I will remember this and I can remember where he winds up. I am just kind of riding along in between. Open, close, start and stop. One secondary promise at a time. Always connecting them back to the primary promise. Because if you do four or five secondaries, they forgot the primary. If you don't keep making that hook. So the mistakes most often made in the middle are, number one, the whole thing is just too damn complicated. The people that make this mistake the most are people who for one reason or another feel compelled to teach. They have convinced themselves for one reason or another. Like gee, these people came to a seminar. So we should give them a seminar. Now they came to a seminar but you are doing sales presentation. Those are two entirely different things. They came to an interview as a webcast. Yes, but you are doing a sales presentation. So teachers, people who like to teach, feel some ethical guilt ridden need to teach. For whatever reason, the teachers get themselves in real trouble. Because they make the whole thing way too complicated that anybody is ready for at their first exposure. The guy you saw in my video sales letter, Phil Grove. So Phil, first of all, he is an engineer. He is an engineer background. So he thinks like an engineer. And he sounds like an engineer. But that is reassuring to a lot of people. I like that part of him. But turned loose in a preview seminar, he teaches. And he teaches all the complicated stuff. 37 ways to make a deal, he tries to teach all 37 of them. I am going Phil. They can't take it. That is years worth of coaching. They just want to know that you got 37 ways and they can get a deal. We don't need to teach them. It is in the way. So you can make it way too complicated. So people always ask me, would I do it differently today? Why did in the whole magnetic marketing speech, once we get to media, the only example I show is direct mail and mostly I talk about direct mail. Reason for that. They can't handle more than one media. If I start talking to them about direct mail and email and broadcast facts and everything, I have just made it way too complicated to make the sale. It is like, why three steps? There is no magic in the three steps. It can be five. It can be 47, as you guys all know. A, three is a nice number. People can remember three. I have a whole framework of three in that speech. Message, market, match. Three step, well, a whole series of threes. People can remember threes. It also works with the Dunning Letter story, the collection industry story, which is three steps. But not everybody uses three steps. So here is how you do a three step and then if you want to do a five step and you could do a 17 step and you could integrate email and you could integrate phone calls, I'm not going to sell shit. I sold because they got it. Okay, three steps. A, I could probably do three steps. It's a little taxing, but I could probably do three. I haven't run out of fingers when I count them. I've got fingers left over. I kind of get it. So you can over complicate the middle. Too many ideas, too much information, too much teaching. And to play fair about this, for the most part, there's very little that you can do in one presentation of any length that the overwhelming majority of people are actually going to be able to take, go and implement and get any results for. You're smoking crack. They can't do it. So I know Tony claims he can, you know, touch them on the head and give them an organism and change their lives forever and God bless them. But two days later, they're right back to where they were. The best we can do is we can create awareness of new method, new opportunity, new solution, new way of thinking about something. We can create interest in it, and then we can provide some sort of opportunity to implement over time, whether that's buying a toolkit or being so. So from a playing fair standpoint, you do no one any favors by just burying their butt in information. They won't go use it anyway. They can't. My theft control speaker, when I got him as a client, I took his one day seminar and I made three days out of it. He said, Jack, you got it. They can't. They're lost by the time you're at one o'clock in the afternoon. So they get lost. That's the second big mistake. So when things aren't hooked together for them, if you leave it up to them to make the connections, they will get lost. So you need structure because they need structure. Too much information, not enough emotion. They only need to know enough to buy. The rest of the time ought to be motivating them about buying, either by scaring the crap out of them, if you wish, or painting them the wonderful picture of how things will be. So whether you like it or not, if you are a group presenter, you are a motivational speaker. You may not like the term any more than I like the term, but that's what you are. So they only need to know enough to buy. They don't need to know any more than that. You gain no leverage by giving them any more knowledge than what they need to buy. All you do is waste time, cost yourself money, and deprive them of whatever opportunity it is that you are presenting to them. So you've got to get to emotion. No fun. Buying is supposed to be fun. People shop for fun. You might not, I might not, say Walmart's entire business is because it is an entertainment for low income people. And that's, by the way, why their sales right now have taken a big dip. Because low income people have less discretionary money. The price of gas is up, the price of food is up. So a higher percentage of their income is going to basic necessities. So now they go to the Walmart store three times a week, and they actually pretty much only get what they went there to get that they need, and they go home. So they get toilet paper, and they get some frozen pizzas, and they get a bottle of juice, and they go home. Walmart's whole business is about them coming three times a week to get those things, but then being there for an hour and a half has their entertainment. They wander through the store, and they find a DVD, and they might want to watch, and they find a new kitchen gadget, and they find, that's their entertainment. And so shopping for them is fun. For you going to Neiman Marcus might be fun. Or going to Nordstrom's might be fun. For me, going to a horse auction is fun. I'm shopping, I'm buying. Oh good, I feel good because I bought and I had fun. The reason Disney sells so much stuff is because people have fun while they are buying. They have completely integrated shopping with entertainment. The whole thing. You can't get out of an attraction without going through the store. The whole thing is one giant, the World Showcase. How many of you have been to the World Showcase part of Epcot? Let's do it the other way. How many have been? Really? Oh, that's the guy. Where are you people doing? How can that be? So you got to go. So World Showcase is a bunch of countries. So I explain this every once in a while to Carla. It would be ever so nice to go over. See we can go to Orlando and in half a day we can do 22 countries. How many more do you need to visit? We just zoop zoop zoop zoop zoop zoop zoop zoop zoop zoop zoop. We can be at the pool in the afternoon if you want to. You know, you go to the spa and go get a massage. We knocked out Europe, Asia, you know, Africa. We blew them all out, you know. So World Showcase is countries. That's what it is. Little miniature landmark, little main street village area of Paris and London and Istanbul and, you know, whatever else. It stores their gift shops. That's what they are. There's one movie in each one. That's the only thing that's there that isn't about buying stuff. Then there's restaurants and there's stores. And it's all imported stuff from the country that it represents. So, you know, everything we bought at Harrods in London and Schlutt Home and Drug Through Customs and all that stuff I could buy right there in England. I've proven it. Right there in England and Disney. They'll ship it to my house and they'll throw in a free Mickey Mouse watch. So the whole thing is shopping. They have made a day of shopping and they have people confused. They don't realize they're shopping even. They think they're being entertained and they're having a ball. And somehow they wind up with 18 bags full of stuff. Right, because that's what it is. It's ingenious. It's a mall that nobody really thinks of as a mall. That's what it is. It's clever. So people buy more happily when they have some fun doing it. So your webcast, your webinar, your meeting ought not be too serious from beginning to end. It's okay to have a little fun with people. If they're smart customers, it's even okay to let them have a little inside baseball. To let them understand a little bit about what's happening to them. So there ought to be fun. Another mistake is that they actually tell things and tell too much about things that they raise doubts rather than create reassurance. So when Phil teaches the 37 ways that you can get a deal, he isn't reassuring the people we put in the room. He's raising a bunch of doubts in the people we put in the room. Because now it sounds really complicated. And how am I going to pick which ones? Do I have to do all of them? And all of the technical language comes out of his mouth. Appraisals and property evaluations and lenders and the federal law, 1803 slash XYZ42 that was passed that covers land trusts. Everybody's going, wait a minute. I thought this was big money and big chunks. This don't sound like big money and big chunks to me. Uh oh, this guy's a rocket scientist. I'm going, nah, nah, nah, nah, we need to reassure people. They need to feel ever more secure that they can do this, that it's painless, that you can figure it out, that you're going to have all this help. So a big mistake is actually raising doubt rather than further erasing doubt. So next category of stuff has to do with bridges and segues. I'm on 86, so a lot of people ask about this. I just picked off my old friend Steve Miller's question, but a lot of people ask, how do you transition from primary content to selling? That's his question. So the first part of that answer is, you don't because you should have been selling the entire time. The idea that there is a border, so you got to be put in here. You got to understand no borders, right? You guys only thought there was a border. So the idea that there is a border of somehow between primary content and selling, all that does is get you in trouble. The worst version of it is the person who delivers content, content, content, content, content, content, content, content, content, content, content, content, content, content. And then they sort of physiologically change, and now they deliver a commercial. Here's what happens when the person has a choice. So that's TV. Entertainment, entertainment, entertainment, commercial. What does everybody do when the commercial comes on? They go get food, they go get a beer, they go to the bathroom and get rid of the food. So on an X break, they can get some more food. So they're either in the kitchen or they're in the can every time the commercials come on, unless they have a way to zoop past them because they recorded the show instead of watching the show live. The only time anybody voluntarily, so some people are too lazy to get up off the couch, so they sit there, but the only time people voluntarily want to see the commercials is when? Super Bowl. Yeah, there's a ton of people who now skip the game. So they yack, they make sandwiches, they go to the can during the game, and then all the commercials are on. The last four years I've been someplace where people watch the Super Bowl, you actually hear it in the house. Hey, hey, the commercials are on, come in, everybody runs in to watch the commercials. I'm going, I thought this was a football game. You know, and I hate halftime. So, so if it doesn't work for TV, why would you do it to yourself? So what happens if you do content, content, content, whoops, commercial, is they mentally disconnect right then. And so they go to the mental toilet and they think about something completely unrelated to what you've been talking about and incidentally start forgetting all of the points you made at a rapid pace. So they now go think about sex or they think about the leaky faucet or they think about whatever, right, they mentally leave. And when you stop giving the commercial and you start telling a story or something, they sort of, these back to content. So that's no good. Furthermore, the thought that there are boundaries is a false thought. Every one to many selling presentation is a selling presentation. Does it have content in it? Yes. Are people capable of confusing it to our advantage? Yes, just like people who spend half a day in the country, is a Disney. Don't think they're in a shopping mall, right? People can see your presentation and think, think they're in a content presentation. So we got two things from my presentations on the success events during the nine years that I did them, white mail about them. Most of which went to Peter first. So we got complaints more than any other speaker, by the way. So I consistently held the record in the most of the people who would take the time to write a letter to complain about something. So I got those, a whole variety, but most of them predictable. You could guess. I also got the most number of letters complaining essentially about the entire event by praising the fact that finally this guy we never heard of at the end of the day gave us more content than anybody else all added together. I have a big stack of them. I used to send the ones that specifically reference Zigg. I used to send them to Zigg with a post it note on it said, see? Once we were buddies. So people thought they got more content from my presentation than they got from any other presentation. Not so. Especially not usable content. I mean, you got to be really astute to leave from that presentation and go home and do anything. You got to be sharp as a tack. But because I did all the stuff we talked about earlier with ideas that stick. I did numbers. I did all this stuff. See, they thought they were getting content. My goal was to give them not one minute of usable content. I'm there to sell stuff. I'm very clear about why I'm there. I made that mistake my first year of speaking and I got it completely out of my system. So I thought if you just give them a ton of great content, they can clearly see you have taught them 512 secrets of the meaning of life on this planet. You can then just say over there some of my stuff and they'll all run over there and get it. So I made that mistake. A lot of people never outgrow that mistake, by the way. They keep trying to make the content better, thinking that that will cause people to be smart enough to now buy their stuff. People ain't that smart. They're just not. So all you do with that is defeat you and defeat them. So I made that mistake. I made the mistake of trying to do the mix and have the borders and all that. And about a year I got it all out of my system. And I said, I don't care. 90 minutes, 4 hours, 8 hours, whatever you want me to do. When I sit down and build it, if I build a new one or I tweak one I've got, I'm building a sales presentation. That's what I'm building. The main way to do it so people can't tell the difference is by telling them what to do and what not to do, but never telling them how to do it. And then just continuing to reinforce, show and demonstrate the what to do and the what not to do, but you never really get to the how to do it. So if you go to Magnetic Marketing Speech you see the whole conversation within message about a unique selling proposition and how you need a unique selling proposition and the fact that nobody has one. And here's a great example which is Domino's. And when I'm all done, here's what people know. They know they need one. They know they ain't got one. They have no earthly idea how to go sit down with a pad and a pen and build one. None, zero, nada. I didn't give them any templates. I didn't give them 22 examples. I didn't tell them what should be first and what should be second. I didn't do any of that. But they think they got all kinds of stuff. So that's what you want to do. So there's no boundaries. There are, however, needed bridges and segues from one place to another, from what is perceived to be content to what will now be perceived as call to action. We do need to help them get from place to place. But you ought to be very clear that you're actually moving them from a selling place to another selling place. You are not moving them from a content place to a selling place. There is no content place. There's only a content place they think is a content place. And Disney actually does put a movie in every one of these places and it's generosity on their part. Because they could just as easily have another store in there and everybody would be just as happy, by the way. So we're bridging and seguing from selling to selling to selling. Some people will feel this is unfair. And again, remind you, the least fair thing in the world is to get somebody all lathered up about fixing a problem or capitalizing on an opportunity and send them home away from you, lathered up with no means of following through. What could be less fair than that? So if you don't sell them your thing, you wasted their time. So you got to get past any attitudinal things you have about this. So your page 86, here's how we do bridges and segues. The difference basically is that a segue is an uninterrupted transition. It feels completely seamless. Nobody would ever really recognize that you were moving them. A bridge is a structural device and mostly you are acknowledging, here's a bridge, come with me. We are now going from here to here. That's really the difference. The thing you're accomplishing is the same. So how to get from the end of an idea to the next idea, some best options. One, a bridge that is a summary statement plus the introduction of the next idea. Now that we have eradicated bad breath, if you want to have a lot of sex, we must also eradicate body odor. Now we have moved from one to the other and we're going to sell a box of stuff that includes mouthwash and deodorant and soap. Another bridge structure is trial close, that their response becomes the reason for being. Now that we have shown you how to eliminate bad breath, if you can have great breath all the time, wouldn't you love also to have a wonderful smelling body so you can actually get laid? Yes, I would. Segway is a mini story that ties idea A to idea B. So we could not tell a story about the person who now has wonderful breath and still can't get laid. And he now discovered actually that the better his breath spells, the more noticeable it is that his body stinks. And so we have to fix both these problems. Segway is a mini demo. I'll stop. A mini demo that ties idea A to idea B. I was good at doing it. Segway, no, we don't really have time in here. The last is a testimonial that ties idea A to idea B. So the testimonial completes the loop of the first idea and raises the second idea. How to get from one segment to the next, you build a bridge of a summary plus a tease of what's coming up next. So it's perfectly fine to say, okay, that wraps up X. Now, here's the next amazing thing. You're good at discover. So that wraps up how to get money in big, big money and big chunks from easy transactions. Now we're going to talk about how to multiply the money you get through investing. It's perfectly okay to say it. To let them get okay. We built this box. We filled it up with cool stuff. We all now understand it and know it. What you're really standing, say, is against you now know how to build a great marketing message. They don't, but they'll nod and say to themselves they do because I just summarized it and told them they do. And now we're going to talk about how to figure out who you should deliver the message to. New box. It's fine to do that. It's done a lot. So when you're actually going from content to commercial and it is going to be noticeable. So now we finally get to the answer Steve wanted. So if you're actually going to go from content to commercial in a way that is noticeable. Now, again, keep in mind you're really going from perceived content to commercial. You're never going from real content to commercial because you shouldn't be delivering any real content. But if it's going to be seen, felt, recognized, then here's the way to do it. So number one, which is used more often than anything else, is a simple reason why. And the benefit here is the reason why can include an enormous amount of truth. So the old version of this was the 7 to 21 times repetition arguments stated one way or another. So it was some version of maybe with a trial close. Okay, we've done this, this and this. And aren't you feeling great? You're like, you can't wait to get home and apply this to your isn't if you want to do trial close. Isn't that right? Everybody feels that way. Raise your hand. So now here's the bad news. And now there's a thing about how bad your memory is, which it's terrible. So X percentage of you three days from now, you will have forgotten everything I said except for the jokes. And seven days from now, you won't remember who I was. And 14 days from now, you won't remember having been here, let alone what the hell we talked about. And those of you that took a bunch of notes, here's what happens with notes. You go home, you got a place. Everybody's got a place where they put all the notes from all the seminars they go to. It's either under the bed, it's out in the garage in boxes. It's in the nice boxes you get at office max that you have to assemble yourself with the lids. You got a whole stack of them somewhere with all the notes that came to the seminars. And it's where the notes from this seminar go to. So therefore, now we have to solve that problem for people, right? So the way we used to solve it was, here's all the stuff you need to listen to, and you need to listen to it seven to 21 times, and then it'll be permanently embedded in your psychology, and you'll be able to, so we don't tell that anymore, because if I tell you you have to listen to it seven to 21 times, you don't buy it. It's hard enough to get you to buy it to listen to it once, because you really just want the thing done for you. So the new hook to that is more the toolkit. So it's either tools or it's done for you. So the solution now to this problem, that you are going to leave here all lathered up, and you think you got it, but you don't, and by the time you get where you are that you're going to try and use it, you will not remember how to use it. So if we just taught you how to fix your kids, and how to have happy, healthy, respectful teenagers who eagerly clean their room and don't cuss at you anymore, and here's the scripts, and by the time you actually get a chance to use it on a teenager, you won't remember how to do it, and you'll bungle it all. So here's the solution to that. When we self-fix the phone, my client Jay Geyer, we make a big point out of, okay, now you got it, right? You understand how horrible it is, and you understand what a good phone call is supposed to sound like, and all that, but if you go try and do this yourself, here's what's going to happen. The staff is going to either quit or they are going to kill you, right? Because you will bungle it. Sometimes we'll tell the Mike Vance piss-in-the-pool story, which Google Vance and then search for piss-in-the-pool if you don't know the story. So what way or another we invalidate what they think they're going to be able to do, and now we have a solution. It used to be spaced repetition learning, then magnetic marketing is all about a toolkit that's so brain dead that you can't help but use it, just page out, use it just the way it is, fill in a few blanks, or if you're selling done for you, it's done for you. If you're selling coaching, it's coaching, but the setup is really the same, so there's a reason why bridge, right? Another bridge is the simplest one, which used to work a lot, is just more, right? You had a good time, we had a ball, we just did this two-day seminar on well, guess what? There's a 36-module, 12-month thing, more of it, you want more of it, you want to take me home with you, to be with you night and day, don't you? So there's a more argument, right? There's the if bridge. I use this a lot when I'm selling, so it's really a series of little trial closes, I don't usually make people raise their hands, but there's the if you have seen great value, if you have all gotten at least one million dollar idea that you're good at, then there is more. So there's the if bridge, there's the in-your-service bridge, which is now, it links to the reason why, but it's the reason why that I'm about to sell you this stuff is because I am deeply concerned that you are going to be able to implement, and I will lose sleep and perhaps over time become suicidal, if I think, that all I'm doing is getting you weathered up and leaving you unable to implement. So it's sort of the mission statement thing. There's the divide and conquer bridge, which is some version of we reached the point where everybody is going to identify themselves, and there are really X kinds of people, and they reveal themselves by X kinds of responses to where we are at this moment. So there's this kind of people, and they are already at the back of the room trying to find the resources, right? Then there's these kinds of, so it's these kind of people. So the first time I really saw it platform selling, and I saw divide and conquer, it blew me away. And it was Mr. Gentile lovable Zig Ziglar who did it, and it is brutally manipulative. And I was in awe, and I was very young, but I got it instantly. He's speaking at a huge Amway rally. Now you have to understand, multi-level, everybody recruits somebody else. That's the deal. It's not like direct selling where maybe there's two levels of it. MLM, everybody's recruiting. And at the big rallies, everybody's got somebody with them, unless they're a real loser, who they are either trying to recruit or they have just recruited. So they're there with somebody who they are trying to lead. So Zig's clothes is, you notice if you turn around and look, while we've been doing this, facilitate the lines at the back of the room, they have put up three different color flags. And they have. They put up these flags. I watched them do it. And they're the kind that used to have gas stations or car washes. They're the triangle flags on a string, red, white, and blue. It's great. So that's because there's three lines back there to get three different packages of resources. So you want to be in the white line. If you're just kind of messing around with this, you don't really know if you're going to get serious. You're really kind of a spare time, make a few extra bucks. Not that there's anything wrong with that. He said it differently than me, but not much. You want to be in the white line. And you're going to get this. He didn't mention price, by the way. Now, if you're a little more serious and you know that you want to build a business and you're going to be an organizational leader and you're going to help other people build. But you want to get in the blue line and you're going to get this. Now, if you're a real leader and you're really going to lead people to new lives of prospects, you're going to get under the red flags and you're going to get this. So exactly who is going to get in the white flag, right? The equivalent of what he's really saying is, if you have a really, really, really tiny dick. And you don't mind everybody knowing it. Get in the white line. That's what he's really saying. I said that to him, I told him this whole story. And he said this, I said, so my translation of what you said, I would never say that. He probably would have sold a little more merchandise. So nobody's going to get in the white line, right? So actually, this day, there were about 8, 10 people who got in the white line. They all moved over. They like, moved over, right? And a bunch of the people in the blue line who started in the blue line, they moved over. Because the longest line is the red line. How can you be there with somebody you're supposed to lead and not get in the leader line? You've got to get in the leader line. It's sales by extortion. It was magnificent. I watched this. I'm a kid. I'm watching this. This is the coolest pickpocket thing scheme. He's left them no choice with this. This is the greatest thing I've ever seen, right? So I've never done it with the lines. I would love to do it with the lines. But I've never done it with the lines. But you do it verbally, and you can do it as the bridge, right? So some ways to soften the bridge, should you wish to do so. One is a summary story between the middle and the bridge. So if you look at the magnetic marketing speech, the Alda Plummer story is between the middle and the bridge to a visible commercial, to a visible call to action. And the story does several things. It summarizes all of the things I told them they're going to be able to do in the context of one story. It's funny, so it softens the transition into the hard CTA, and it sets up the reason to have the resources. You can do a summary testimonial between the middle and the bridge. So instead of a story, it can be a story told about a person which summarizes the main points you made and gets us into the bridge, or it can be in that person's voice. You could show a video, you could go to a video clip in a webinar of a testimonial in order to set up the bridge. You can put your mission story here, and you can do it as a blackie story if you like. So Andrew Carnegie is a blackie in Napoleon Hill story, so this could be how you were called out, for example, on your dereliction of duty. We're running around doing these presentations and getting a standing ovation and going on your merry way and relying on people to go kind of figure it out. And blackie, whoever he may be, the CEO of this company, this big company, this highly successful CEO, or this wise elder person who makes all the money in the real estate business, went and grabbed you by the scruff of the neck and took you into a closet and beat you with a rubber hose and said to you, how dare you leave all these people with no resources and no tools. And so you got it and you accepted the responsibility and you went back and you created all these tools so nobody would ever have to struggle unnecessarily. Again, I have a blackie story I sometimes use about the, actually I have two, they're really sort of the same purpose. There's a blackie story, it's true, but so I was speaking for the continental foods, they sell the restaurants, they're a commercial food vendor. So they're the guys that sell food here, Hilton would buy it from continental or there's only four or five companies. So I was speaking to continental foods, Southwest region in Phoenix, and they had just got a new vice president of sales. I had already been booked. And so I'm speaking and I'm selling magnetic market. And you can set this up sometimes, but I didn't set it up. The guy spontaneously three quarters of the way through the commercial, he interrupted it, which is usually not good. But he said, look, we feel this is important enough for everybody to have, we'll pay half. And so you only pay half and you only pay half, we'll pay half. And if you like, we'll take it out of your commission checks over the next six weeks, so you want to feel it. And the guy goes and sits back down, I finished the patch and people buy it. So I'm packing up my stuff, everybody's gone to lunch and he comes and says, can we get copies of the order forms? I said, of course. He said, can I get the list right now who bought, you know, I said, sure. And he says, because I'm going to fire everybody who didn't. This was his litmus test to quickly determine who was serious about succeeding and who wasn't. So he could get rid of all the deadwood without having to spend six weeks figuring it out. Now, I will use that story to put the fear of God into people about buying. There's another version of it about Alex Gohan bringing me in to do training and consulting at Trim Spa. And he had assigned the ultimate sales letter book to everybody before I got there. And he popped a quiz on it before I started. And one guy confessed to not having had time to read the book. And Alex said, then you don't have time to work here either out. The story serves the same purpose. So if you look at your page 89, I'll quickly show you where this stuff is in the old script. So we're back to 1999. Never really changed, though. So here's a bridge between idea sections on 89. Well, for the sake of our limited time, because you always want to imply there's more shit you could have given them, but you can't. Let's assume we figure out this first thing. Let's assume we have a powerful marketing message. We're ready to get it out to the marketplace. So you got that now. I showed it to you. I taught it to you. You know how to do it. You got a powerful marketing message. Now we're going to move on to the next challenge, which is the world's greatest marketing message, no better than the world's worst marketing message if it shouted at deaf ears, wasting bullets on targets. So now we're going to talk about target marketing. So I bridged from one to the other. I also made a factually untrue statement that you now have this, so we're going to go do this. That's really what you got is an awareness of this and a knowledge that you don't have it. That's really what you got, right? Page 90, you'll see another bridge down at the bottom. So let's assume for the sake of our conversation, we figured two things out. So now I'm going back in case they forgot the first one was message. I'm telling them it's message. So the second thing is, now here's the third issue. How do we take this marketing message implication? You've already got it. We have so lovingly crafted and birthed and delivered over these prospects. We have so carefully selected. How do we get it to them? So now we are going to talk about media. So I bridged each time with an untrue statement. And the reason is that essentially what I've said is, okay, we got your breath fixed, but you're still not going to get laid because your body stinks. So we got to move over here now for this to be any good to you. You also need this. And now that we got these two things, now it's your personality. So now, long way to an old joke. Page 91 now has a segue to the summary story. And it tells them why I'm going to tell them the story, what they should look for in the story, what the story's all about. Now I'm going to tell you one final story. This story keeps all of the promises I made to you at the beginning of our time together. And then I tell them everything that's going to be in the story. And I give them the specific instructions. See, if you take this and you go back through everything we've talked about. So it ends with a specific instruction. Stay tuned. Pay attention for it to do all of those things. All the promises I just restated. Every little nuance is important. I only have time to tell it once. You've got to listen close. So there's a specific instruction there. If you look at page 92, here's the problem bridge. Memory argument. There are problems with everything we do here this afternoon, even if we had twice as much time, 20 times more time. I'd just solve them in seven minutes. I'm telling them now that the call to action here, the real commercial is going to be seven minutes long. It ain't seven minutes. It's longer than seven minutes. But they feel better that there's only seven minutes that's coming. You've said to yourself, baby, maybe there's so that you've got the whole problem solution thing here, which you can study as you wish. But if in it, so on page 93, so if you said maybe, then these seven minutes are of concern to you because here are the problems. It's got just about everything I just described to you in it. The bridge, the gap to the tools. And finally, there we are.
